topic: Why Venus Became Hell and Earth Could Too
title: The Runaway Greenhouse That Can Turn a Planet into a Furnace

lesson.learn:
Venus became so extreme because sunlight, atmospheric chemistry, and feedback loops pushed it into a runaway greenhouse state. Once the planet heated enough, water vapor became a stronger greenhouse gas, which trapped even more heat and accelerated the process. Over time, that runaway cycle created a world with crushing pressure, toxic clouds, and surface temperatures hot enough to melt lead.

Earth is not Venus, but the comparison matters. A planet’s climate can cross a tipping point if feedbacks outrun its ability to cool itself back down.
lesson.simpler: Venus got too hot because heat trapped more heat. If feedback keeps growing, a planet can turn into a furnace.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Runaway greenhouse feedback can transform a potentially habitable planet into an oven.
lesson.deeperDive:
The physics is not mystical. A warmer atmosphere can hold more water vapor, and water vapor itself traps heat. Add thick carbon dioxide clouds, a weak water cycle, and long-term solar input, and the planet loses the stable balance that Earth enjoys today. That is why Venus is a warning rather than just a curiosity.

Earth has stronger stabilizing systems, including oceans, weathering, and a biosphere that changes the atmosphere. Even so, climate feedbacks matter because planetary habitability depends on keeping those balancing mechanisms intact.
lesson.funFact: Venus’s surface is hot enough to melt lead.

people: David Grinspoon - Venus and habitability researcher
places: Venus orbit and atmosphere - runaway greenhouse case study
NASA planetary science labs - climate modeling
Earth climate research centers - feedback analysis
years: 1960s - Venus becomes a planetary science focus
concepts: Runaway greenhouse - heat feedback that amplifies itself
Albedo - how much sunlight a planet reflects
Water vapor feedback - warming caused by more humidity
Atmospheric pressure - the weight of the air above
